title: Characterizing quantum correlations and quantum teleportation in $gg \to t\bar{t}$ and $q\bar{q} \to t\bar{t}$ processes under noisy channels
authors: Elhabib Jaloum, Omar Bachain, Mohamed Amazioug, Nazek Alessa, Rachid Ahl Laamara, R. T. Matoog, Abdel-Haleem Abdel-Aty
abstract: The measurement of top-quark spin correlations provides a key tool for probing its interactions with high precision. Owing to its extremely short lifetime ($τ\sim 10^{-25}$ s), the top quark preserves its spin polarization information, making the $t\bar{t}$ system an ideal framework for investigating quantum correlations in high-energy physics. In this work, we analyze quantum correlations in $t\bar{t}$ pairs produced in QCD using several quantum information-theoretic measures, including Bell nonlocality, quantum steering, concurrence, and geometric quantum discord. Their dependence on kinematic variables is examined in both the $gg \to t\bar{t}$ and $q\bar{q} \to t\bar{t}$ channels, with convergence toward the $gg \to t\bar{t}$ dominated regime in the ultra-relativistic limit ($β= 1$). We also investigate the effect of three effective decoherence channels (AD, PD, and PF). The AD and PD channels lead to a monotonic degradation of correlations as the decoherence parameter $p$ increases, while the PF channel exhibits a symmetric behavior around $p=1/2$. The impact of these channels on quantum teleportation is analyzed, showing that it remains above the classical threshold of $2/3$ even in the presence of noise. These results indicate that certain quantum resources can persist despite decoherence, opening new perspectives at the interface of quantum information and particle physics.
summary: This paper investigates how quantum correlations and teleportation fidelity in top-antitop quark production are affected by different types of environmental noise. It shows that certain quantum resources, such as geometric quantum discord, are more robust to decoherence than entanglement or Bell nonlocality. The study highlights the potential for maintaining quantum advantages even in noisy, high-energy particle physics processes.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the dynamics and robustness of various quantum correlations (Bell nonlocality, steering, entanglement, and discord) and quantum teleportation fidelity in top-antitop (t-tbar) quark production under the influence of environmental noise.
main_result: The researchers found that while AD and PD channels cause monotonic decay of correlations, the PF channel allows for a partial revival of correlations, and quantum teleportation fidelity remains above the classical threshold of 2/3 even in the presence of noise.
method: The authors use quantum information-theoretic measures (Bell-CHSH, CJWR inequality, Concurrence, and GQD) applied to a spin density matrix derived from QCD production channels, modeling decoherence via Kraus operator formalism for AD, PD, and PF channels.
model_or_system: The system consists of top-antitop (t-tbar) quark pairs produced via gluon-gluon fusion (gg to t-tbar) and quark-antiquark annihilation (q-qbar to t-tbar), modeled as a bipartite spin-1/2 system described by an X-state density matrix.
key_observables: Bell nonlocality, quantum steering, concurrence, geometric quantum discord (GQD), and quantum teleportation fidelity.
important_parameters: Top-quark velocity (beta), production angle (theta), decoherence parameter (p), and the ultra-relativistic limit (beta = 1).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled as a bipartite spin-1/2 system; the analysis focuses on leading-order QCD production and specific effective decoherence channels.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of quantum correlations on production angles and velocity, the decay of correlations under different noise channels, and the behavior of teleportation fidelity as a function of the decoherence parameter and phase angles.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the top-quark spin system, defines the QCD production channels and noise models, quantifies various quantum correlations, analyzes the impact of decoherence on these correlations, and evaluates the efficiency of quantum teleportation.
why_interesting: This paper is interesting for researchers in open quantum systems as it applies standard decoherence models (AD, PD, PF) to a high-energy particle physics context, demonstrating how quantum resources like discord and teleportation fidelity can persist despite environmental noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Bell nonlocality as a function of the top-quark velocity β and the production angle θ. (a) Gluon-fusion channel gg →t¯t; (b) top-antitop quark annihilation channel q¯q →t¯t, production at the LHC for pp collisions at √s = 13Tev
FIG. 2: Quantum steering as a function of the top-quark velocity β and the production angle θ. (a) Gluon-fusion channel gg →t¯t; (b) top- antitop annihilation channel q¯q →t¯t.
FIG. 3: Concurrence as a function of the top-quark velocity β and the production angle θ. (a) Gluon-fusion channel gg →t¯t; (b) top-antitop quark annihilation channel q¯q →t¯t.
FIG. 4: Geometric quantum discord as a function of the top-quark velocity β and the production angle θ. (a) Gluon-fusion channel gg →t¯t; (b) top-antitop quark annihilation channel q¯q →t¯t.

FIG. 5: Quantum correlations in the presence of decoherence: Bell nonlocality, quantum steering, concurrence, and geometric quantum discord as functions of the production angle θ and the decoherence parameter p. Results are shown for β = 1 in the (a) amplitude-damping (AD), (b) phase-damping (PD) and (c) phase-flip (PF) channels. The limit β = 1 corresponds to the regime where the q¯q →t¯t process approaches the gluon-initiated channel gg →t¯t.
